reason: The item cleanly isolates a single judgment axis—whether explosion risk at this SOC is more consistent with pressure-rise/relief dynamics versus surface-heat accumulation—using a counterintuitive temperature observation to enforce boundary-aware reasoning. Decoys are technically plausible and mostly single-fault (causal inversion, scope/measurement overclaim, and cross-geometry extrapolation), supporting strong discrimination without relying on stylistic cues. D5 is slightly capped because two correct options (C and G) are semantically close, reducing marginal separation among expert choices.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware safety judgment in confined deployment by separating thermal stability indicators from overpressure/mechanical hazards and incorporating SOC-dependent nonlinearity. Correct options are tightly supported without requiring unstated background knowledge, while incorrect options each hinge on a single, realistic extrapolation error (overgeneralization, scope expansion, or single-cause mechanism attribution). Discrimination is strong for experts because it punishes common heuristic shortcuts, though some options use absolutist language that may slightly ease elimination.

The correct options are tightly anchored to explicit timing/threshold constraints and avoid overclaiming beyond the stem’s stated detection limits and reaction onset temperatures. Decoys are plausible and each mostly fails by a single, identifiable reasoning error (timing-insensitive adequacy and overstrong causal negation). The only notable quality drag is that the item bundles two distinct subproblems (detection-choice plus ISC mechanistic inference/parameter transfer), slightly reducing coherence and potentially enabling partial-answering strategies.

The item cleanly targets a boundary-aware mechanistic distinction: pressure-rise/relief dynamics versus temperature-based proxies, plus a constrained interpretation of SOC-dependent gas composition trends. It offers strong discriminative value by tempting a common but invalid proxy (peak surface temperature) while anchoring the correct reasoning in the provided pressure-rate contrast and the stated CO/HC trend. Minor neutrality risk remains because the prompt foregrounds the pressure-rise difference so prominently that some solvers may shortcut to the intended critique without fully engaging the mechanistic chain.
